feedback1: Error bars should be included in Figure 2 D.
feedback2: The model assumes only one kind of role for fake news dissemination, which underestimates the complexity of real-world scenarios where there are both intentional creators of fake news and unintentional spreaders. The authors should consider more related work in information diffusion that addresses competitive propagation with multiple parties.
title: Modeling Fake News in Social Networks with Deep Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
abstract: We develop a practical and flexible computational model of fake news on social networks in which agents act according to learned best response functions. We achieve this by extending an information aggregation game to allow for fake news and by representing agents as recurrent deep Q-networks (DQN) trained by independent Q-learning. In the game, agents repeatedly guess whether a claim is true or false taking into account an informative private signal and observations of actions of their neighbors on the social network in the previous period. We incorporate fake news into the model by adding an adversarial agent, the attacker, that either provides biased private signals to or takes over a subset of agents. The attacker can follow either a hand-tuned or trained policy. Our model allows us to tackle questions that are analytically intractable in fully rational models, while ensuring that agents follow reasonable best response functions. Our results highlight the importance of awareness, privacy and social connectivity in curbing the adverse effects of fake news.

feedback1: What is the effect of using a frozen SphereHead decoder and the additional loss? Does this constraint cause the triplane features to behave similarly to SphereHead, or are there additional benefits such as improved training stability or convergence speed?
feedback2: It is unclear whether the Frozen SphereHead Decoder is used only during training for the distillation loss or if it is required during inference. Clarification on the inference-time compute graph regarding the SphereHead branch is needed.
title: PanoLAM: Large Avatar Model for Gaussian Full-Head Synthesis from One-shot Unposed Image
abstract: We present a feed-forward framework for Gaussian full-head synthesis from a single unposed image. Unlike previous work that relies on time-consuming GAN inversion and test-time optimization, our framework can reconstruct the Gaussian full-head model given a single unposed image in a single forward pass. This enables fast reconstruction and rendering during inference. To mitigate the lack of large-scale 3D head assets, we propose a large-scale synthetic dataset from trained 3D GANs and train our framework using only synthetic data. For efficient high-fidelity generation, we introduce a coarse-to-fine Gaussian head generation pipeline, where sparse points from the FLAME model interact with the image features by transformer blocks for feature extraction and coarse shape reconstruction, which are then densified for high-fidelity reconstruction. To fully leverage the prior knowledge residing in pretrained 3D GANs for effective reconstruction, we propose a dual-branch framework that effectively aggregates the structured spherical triplane feature and unstructured point-based features for more effective Gaussian head reconstruction. Experimental results show the effectiveness of our framework towards existing work.

feedback1: The legend in Figure 2 A & B is confusing; different styles should be used for 'private signal optimal' and 'all signals optimal'.
feedback2: Modeling users as only voting on the truth or false value of a claim is not realistic, since most social networks involve users liking, sharing, etc., and users are motivated by more than just truthfulness.
title: Modeling Fake News in Social Networks with Deep Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
abstract: We develop a practical and flexible computational model of fake news on social networks in which agents act according to learned best response functions. We achieve this by extending an information aggregation game to allow for fake news and by representing agents as recurrent deep Q-networks (DQN) trained by independent Q-learning. In the game, agents repeatedly guess whether a claim is true or false taking into account an informative private signal and observations of actions of their neighbors on the social network in the previous period. We incorporate fake news into the model by adding an adversarial agent, the attacker, that either provides biased private signals to or takes over a subset of agents. The attacker can follow either a hand-tuned or trained policy. Our model allows us to tackle questions that are analytically intractable in fully rational models, while ensuring that agents follow reasonable best response functions. Our results highlight the importance of awareness, privacy and social connectivity in curbing the adverse effects of fake news.

feedback1: The evaluation compares the full model, a randomly pruned model, and the proposed importance-based pruned model. However, this does not fully isolate the role of layer laziness. Additional controls, such as training a model from scratch with reduced depth or fine-tuning only the critical layers while freezing others, would clarify if the performance gain comes from representational dynamics or from generic shallower optimization effects.
feedback2: Highlight the 'Takeaways' sections more prominently, for example by using italics or boxed text, to improve readability and help skimming readers grasp the core findings.
title: The Few Govern the Many:Unveiling Few-Layer Dominance for Time Series Models
abstract: Time series (TS) forecasting plays a vital role in practice, but remains a highly challenging task. The outstanding performance of large-scale models across multiple domains has driven the advancement of large-scale TS models, providing an effective pathway for forecasting task. Performance degradation has been observed in large-scale TS models, demonstrating that bigger is not always better which is a puzzling phenomenon. We trained two categories of large-scale TS models, LLM4TS and TSFMs, across four scales, examining how architecture, model size, data volume and distribution, and training strategies influence model performance. Due to the lack of in-depth studies on representations in large-scale TS models, we examined the evolution of representations from both inter-layer and intra-layer perspectives. Our analysis reveals that only a small subset of layers play a critical role in learning, while the majority contribute minimally—a phenomenon we term few-layer dominance. Building on the insight, we propose a method to identify critical layers, allowing models to achieve performance on par while improving inference efficiency. Validation on existing large-scale TS models confirms the universality of few-layer dominance and the reliability of critical layers identification method.

feedback1: The authors should provide motivation for choosing to model the social network as a Barabási-Albert graph and explain why this is a reasonable modeling choice.
feedback2: The authors should provide results with a heuristic attacker that always lies about the claim. Is it true in practice that agents learn to distrust such an attacker? Can one find a heuristic attacker that behaves equivalently to the learned one, or even build stronger hand-tuned attackers?
title: Modeling Fake News in Social Networks with Deep Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
abstract: We develop a practical and flexible computational model of fake news on social networks in which agents act according to learned best response functions. We achieve this by extending an information aggregation game to allow for fake news and by representing agents as recurrent deep Q-networks (DQN) trained by independent Q-learning. In the game, agents repeatedly guess whether a claim is true or false taking into account an informative private signal and observations of actions of their neighbors on the social network in the previous period. We incorporate fake news into the model by adding an adversarial agent, the attacker, that either provides biased private signals to or takes over a subset of agents. The attacker can follow either a hand-tuned or trained policy. Our model allows us to tackle questions that are analytically intractable in fully rational models, while ensuring that agents follow reasonable best response functions. Our results highlight the importance of awareness, privacy and social connectivity in curbing the adverse effects of fake news.

feedback1: Although the paper identifies the phenomenon of layer laziness, it lacks a theoretical model explaining its underlying mechanism. The analysis is mainly empirical and descriptive, relying on measures of representation similarity and distance without establishing a causal explanation. The paper does not clarify why time-series large Transformers are more susceptible to lazy representation dynamics than models in language or vision domains. Such an understanding is crucial for generalizing the findings.
feedback2: The concept of 'layer inertia' mentioned in the paper should be expanded upon to clarify whether it results from residual connections dominating outputs or from the attention mechanism failing to find new correlations in deeper layers.
title: The Few Govern the Many:Unveiling Few-Layer Dominance for Time Series Models
abstract: Time series (TS) forecasting plays a vital role in practice, but remains a highly challenging task. The outstanding performance of large-scale models across multiple domains has driven the advancement of large-scale TS models, providing an effective pathway for forecasting task. Performance degradation has been observed in large-scale TS models, demonstrating that bigger is not always better which is a puzzling phenomenon. We trained two categories of large-scale TS models, LLM4TS and TSFMs, across four scales, examining how architecture, model size, data volume and distribution, and training strategies influence model performance. Due to the lack of in-depth studies on representations in large-scale TS models, we examined the evolution of representations from both inter-layer and intra-layer perspectives. Our analysis reveals that only a small subset of layers play a critical role in learning, while the majority contribute minimally—a phenomenon we term few-layer dominance. Building on the insight, we propose a method to identify critical layers, allowing models to achieve performance on par while improving inference efficiency. Validation on existing large-scale TS models confirms the universality of few-layer dominance and the reliability of critical layers identification method.

feedback1: The applicability of the proposed DMARL approach is questionable in its current state, as it is not clear how the conclusions would translate to more realistic scenarios of fake news in social networks due to strong assumptions and the use of a toy example.
feedback2: The authors should provide motivation for choosing to model the social network as a Barabási-Albert graph and explain why this is a reasonable modeling choice.
title: Modeling Fake News in Social Networks with Deep Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
abstract: We develop a practical and flexible computational model of fake news on social networks in which agents act according to learned best response functions. We achieve this by extending an information aggregation game to allow for fake news and by representing agents as recurrent deep Q-networks (DQN) trained by independent Q-learning. In the game, agents repeatedly guess whether a claim is true or false taking into account an informative private signal and observations of actions of their neighbors on the social network in the previous period. We incorporate fake news into the model by adding an adversarial agent, the attacker, that either provides biased private signals to or takes over a subset of agents. The attacker can follow either a hand-tuned or trained policy. Our model allows us to tackle questions that are analytically intractable in fully rational models, while ensuring that agents follow reasonable best response functions. Our results highlight the importance of awareness, privacy and social connectivity in curbing the adverse effects of fake news.

feedback1: The architectural novelty of the paper is not clear. The main innovation seems to be reducing high-order tensors into a few irreducible representations and using a neural network to predict the corresponding scalars. However, the spherical-harmonic convolutional design itself appears standard.
feedback2: The paper should explicitly discuss the computational efficiency differences observed between IrredNet and GMTNet, particularly highlighting the better scaling of IrredNet with tensor order and the reasons behind this, such as reduced degrees of freedom or the eSCN architecture's scaling.
title: Symmetry-Guaranteed Prediction of High-Order Tensor Properties for Crystalline Materials via Irreducible Decomposition
abstract: Predicting high-order tensor properties for crystalline materials is crucial for various scientific and engineering applications. Crystal symmetry is one of the primary factors influencing high-order tensor properties, such as elasticity and piezoelectricity, making strict adherence to symmetry constraints essential. However, exactly guaranteeing symmetry compliance remains challenging. Recent approaches rely on enforcing symmetry but often fail to strictly preserve symmetry. In this work, we propose a novel method that guarantees exact symmetry compliance by predicting symmetry-constrained irreducible components of high-order tensors. Specifically, we first develop a computational procedure to identify the basis tensors corresponding to symmetry-constrained irreducible components under various symmetry conditions. This symmetry-constrained basis guarantees that the assembled full tensor strictly adheres to the required symmetry constraints. To predict the numerical values for these irreducible components, we then propose a spherical-harmonic convolutional neural network designed to effectively capture essential high-order tensor information. Extensive experiments validate that our method achieves exact symmetry compliance without compromising prediction accuracy, thereby outperforming state-of-the-art approaches.

feedback1: The paper lacks comparison with other approaches to information aggregation in social networks, such as heuristic or game-theoretic methods, and should either provide experimental comparisons or clear motivation for not including such methods.
feedback2: The authors should provide results with a heuristic attacker that always lies about the claim. Is it true in practice that agents learn to distrust such an attacker? Can one find a heuristic attacker that behaves equivalently to the learned one, or even build stronger hand-tuned attackers?

feedback1: The paper introduces an equivariant graph neural network for tensor property prediction, but it exhibits a notable level of similarity to GMTNet. These similarities need to be discussed.
feedback2: The paper should explicitly discuss the computational efficiency differences observed between IrredNet and GMTNet, particularly highlighting the better scaling of IrredNet with tensor order and the reasons behind this, such as reduced degrees of freedom or the eSCN architecture's scaling.

feedback1: The paper lacks a significant technical contribution to learning algorithms, as it primarily applies existing DMARL techniques to a novel toy domain without proposing improvements to core algorithms.
feedback2: The applicability of the proposed DMARL approach is questionable in its current state, as it is not clear how the conclusions would translate to more realistic scenarios of fake news in social networks due to strong assumptions and the use of a toy example.

feedback1: The results presented in Table 2 for GMTNet might be problematic due to the improper use of the mask-based symmetry enforcement mechanism.
feedback2: The paper should include a discussion explaining why existing methods like GMTNet perform poorly on the trigonal crystal system, highlighting specific symmetry challenges such as the three-fold rotation axis and off-diagonal constraints that are difficult to capture with rotation matrices.

feedback1: The test statistics resemble HSIC. It would be helpful to include comments on the connection to HSIC.
feedback2: It would be interesting to visualize the importance of features at different depths in deep convolutional networks.
title: Probabilistic Connection Importance Inference and Lossless Compression of Deep Neural Networks
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) can be huge in size, requiring a considerable a mount of energy and computational resources to operate, which limits their applications in numerous scenarios. It is thus of interest to compress DNNs while maintaining their performance levels.  We here propose a probabilistic importance inference approach for pruning DNNs. Specifically, we test the significance of the relevance of a connection in a DNN to the DNN’s outputs using a nonparemtric scoring testand keep only those significant ones. Experimental results show that the proposed approach achieves better lossless compression rates than existing techniques

feedback1: The method is evaluated using only 512 adaptation samples, which may favor NEO. Optimization-based TTA methods typically use conservative strategies, and a slightly higher learning rate may improve their performance when adaptation data is limited. This setup may underestimate the performance of other methods.
feedback2: To claim that NEO is a general Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) method, the paper should include experimental results on at least one CNN architecture, such as ResNet-50 or ConvNeXt, because CNNs often use Batch Normalization (BN) unlike ViTs which use Layer Normalization (LN). Showing NEO's performance on BN-based models is important for a more complete comparison with existing TTA baselines that rely on BN statistics.
title: NEO — No-Optimization Test-Time Adaptation through Latent Re-Centering
abstract: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) methods are often computationally expensive, require a large amount of data for effective adaptation, or are brittle to hyperparameters. Based on a theoretical foundation of the geometry of the latent space, we are able to significantly improve the alignment between source and distribution-shifted samples by re-centering target data embeddings at the origin. This insight motivates NEO – a hyperparameter-free fully TTA method, that adds no significant compute compared to vanilla inference. NEO is able to improve the classification accuracy of ViT-Base on ImageNet-C from 55.6\% to 59.2\% after adapting on just one batch of 64 samples. When adapting on 512 samples NEO beats all 7 TTA methods we compare against on ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R and ImageNet-S and beats 6/7 on CIFAR-10-C, while using the least amount of compute. NEO performs well on model calibration metrics and additionally is able to adapt from 1 class to improve accuracy on 999 other classes in ImageNet-C. On Raspberry Pi and Jetson Orin Nano devices, NEO reduces inference time by 63\% and memory usage by 9\% compared to baselines. Our results based on 3 ViT architectures and 4 datasets show that NEO can be used efficiently and effectively for TTA.

feedback1: The main drawback of the approach is its significantly increased computation due to (1) the quadratic complexity of kernel methods, and (2) inability to cache computation among different pairs of nodes (unlike gradient-based approaches, which can compute importance for all node connections in one forward-backward pass). This limits the applicability of the approach to larger models, such as those used for ImageNet, where model compression is more urgently needed. As a result, the significance and impact of the approach is also limited.
feedback2: Were the fine-tuning results in the random pruning experiments performed with random pruning or with your importance metric? For a fair comparison, the treatment of the pruned network should be the same in both cases. Did you try pruning with your method without retraining? The most drastic results should be shown with fine-tuning of both importance-pruned and randomly-pruned networks.
title: Probabilistic Connection Importance Inference and Lossless Compression of Deep Neural Networks
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) can be huge in size, requiring a considerable a mount of energy and computational resources to operate, which limits their applications in numerous scenarios. feedback1: The theoretical claims rely on neural collapse assumptions, but the authors do not empirically verify that ViT models exhibit NC1-NC4 properties (within-class collapse, equiangular tight frame, self-duality, global mean at the origin). The reviewer requests measurement of these neural collapse metrics on the evaluation models, or ablation experiments to show robustness when these assumptions fail. Additionally, the reviewer notes that the class balance assumption is strong and may not hold in real deployments.
feedback2: The paper should empirically verify the presence of Neural Collapse (NC) in the pre-trained ViT models used, by calculating the norm of the global mean and the equiangularity of class means on the clean ImageNet validation set, since training techniques like Label Smoothing, Dropout, and heavy Augmentation may prevent perfect NC.

feedback2: In the introduction, you mention l0 and l1 norm methods, but cite Han et al. (2015), which compares l1 and l2 norms and finds l2 norm to be better overall.
title: Probabilistic Connection Importance Inference and Lossless Compression of Deep Neural Networks
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) can be huge in size, requiring a considerable a mount of energy and computational resources to operate, which limits their applications in numerous scenarios. It is thus of interest to compress DNNs while maintaining their performance levels.  We here propose a probabilistic importance inference approach for pruning DNNs. Experimental results show that the proposed approach achieves better lossless compression rates than existing techniques

feedback1: How does the method perform without using the attention strategy on the groups to pick C_ATT first, then obtain the optimal goal within C_ATT? The 'Impact of Group Selection' shows a curve with 'no group selection', how exactly was this done? Also, in Section 4.2, it is claimed that supervising for the group instead of the goal can eliminate instability from inaccurate value estimation.
feedback2: Define the intrinsic reward function used in experiments precisely, specifying the exact criterion for goal achievement such as distance thresholds, whether in discretized or continuous space, and whether the reward is sparse or shaped.
title: Explore with Dynamic Map: Graph Structured Reinforcement Learning
abstract: In reinforcement learning, a map with states and transitions built based on historical trajectories is often helpful in exploration and exploitation. Even so, learning and planning on such a map within a sparse environment remains a challenge. As a step towards this goal, we propose Graph Structured Reinforcement Learning (GSRL), which utilizes historical trajectories to slowly adjust exploration directions and learn related experiences while rapidly updating the value function estimation. GSRL constructs a dynamic graph on top of state transitions in the replay buffer based on historical trajectories, and develops an attention strategy on the map to select an appropriate goal direction, which decomposes the task of reaching a distant goal state into a sequence of easier tasks. We also leverage graph structure to sample related trajectories for efficient value learning. Results demonstrate that GSRL can outperform the state-of-the-art algorithms in terms of sample efficiency on benchmarks with sparse reward functions.

feedback1: The experiment section should include more important information, such as the kernel bandwidth used for the Gaussian kernel, since this parameter greatly affects performance. Additionally, the main text introducing the proposed statistical test is a bit verbose and dense in unnecessary notations. It could be made more succinct by presenting a high-level idea (removing mean, three-way correlations) first, followed by the final results, and moving some intermediate results to the Appendix.
feedback2: The empirical results could be clearer; the paper lacks baselines for larger models on CIFAR10 and should compare with published results of other algorithms. Additionally, how does the method perform on larger networks like ImageNet? Computational efficiency is mentioned but could be examined in greater detail, including how long it takes to run and how model size affects runtime.
title: Probabilistic Connection Importance Inference and Lossless Compression of Deep Neural Networks
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) can be huge in size, requiring a considerable a mount of energy and computational resources to operate, which limits their applications in numerous scenarios. It is thus of interest to compress DNNs while maintaining their performance levels.  We here propose a probabilistic importance inference approach for pruning DNNs. Specifically, we test the significance of the relevance of a connection in a DNN to the DNN’s outputs using a nonparemtric scoring testand keep only those significant ones. Experimental results show that the proposed approach achieves better lossless compression rates than existing techniques

feedback1: The paper is missing discussion of related works. Although the abstract claims that current RL-based control systems cannot utilize advanced RL methods while considering stability guarantees, there is a substantial existing literature on RL with stability or safety guarantees. For example: Berkenkamp et al. (2017), Cheng et al. (2019), and Choi et al. (2020).
feedback2: The abstract should explicitly state the assumptions made (such as bounded disturbance, bounded modeling error, bounded policy outputs, Lipschitz dynamics), the precise stability guarantees proven (e.g., ISS, local UUB, global UUB with ultimate bounds), and clarify which parts are heuristic (such as iterative training tending to reduce error).
title: Reinforcement Learning for Control with Stability Guarantee
abstract:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved promising performance in complicated system control. 
To overcome this defect, we firstly apply a Lyapunov stable dynamical model as a reference system to fit the real system. Then, we prove that if the state fitting error between the reference and real system are bounded, the real system has Uniformly Ultimately Bounded (UUB) stability guarantee. Motivated by our theoretical analysis, we guide the design of reward functions for RL based on conditions of UUB guarantee for real systems and propose $\textbf{ITSRL}$, an $\textbf{I}$terative $\textbf{T}$raining framework for learning $\textbf{S}$table $\textbf{RL}$ control policy with UUB stability guarantee, by iteratively minimizing the state fitting error between the reference and real system, which can be adapted to various advanced RL methods.  Our evaluation results on three control tasks demonstrate that the proposed ITSRL framework can improve the performance of RL controller under perturbation.

feedback1: The experimental results, while interesting in showing better compression rates on small datasets, are less convincing than results on more complex models and larger datasets such as ImageNet, where model compression is more impactful. Also, test errors on small datasets are easier to achieve and can be significantly improved by tuning optimization settings like learning rates, making these results more preliminary.
feedback2: The empirical results could be clearer; the paper lacks baselines for larger models on CIFAR10 and should compare with published results of other algorithms. Additionally, how does the method perform on larger networks like ImageNet? Computational efficiency is mentioned but could be examined in greater detail, including how long it takes to run and how model size affects runtime.
title: Probabilistic Connection Importance Inference and Lossless Compression of Deep Neural Networks
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) can be huge in size, requiring a considerable a mount of energy and computational resources to operate, which limits their applications in numerous scenarios. feedback1: The paper's presentation is poor, with many unclear technical details. Specifically: there is no clear algorithm corresponding to Figure 1; the definitions of model error and reward are missing from both the algorithms and the experimental results; and notations are inconsistent, such as 'e(t)' in Assumption 1 versus 'e(t,x)' in Theorem 2.
feedback2: Make the ITSRL algorithm precise by providing pseudocode and clear definitions, including how the error e is computed in practice (state difference, one-step prediction error, multi-step rollout error), whether hat x is generated by simulating hat f with the same control inputs, the horizon over which |e| is measured (instantaneous or trajectory), and specifying data collection, reference model update steps, policy update steps, and stopping criteria.
title: Reinforcement Learning for Control with Stability Guarantee
abstract:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved promising performance in complicated system control. 
To overcome this defect, we firstly apply a Lyapunov stable dynamical model as a reference system to fit the real system. Then, we prove that if the state fitting error between the reference and real system are bounded, the real system has Uniformly Ultimately Bounded (UUB) stability guarantee. Motivated by our theoretical analysis, we guide the design of reward functions for RL based on conditions of UUB guarantee for real systems and propose $\textbf{ITSRL}$, an $\textbf{I}$terative $\textbf{T}$raining framework for learning $\textbf{S}$table $\textbf{RL}$ control policy with UUB stability guarantee, by iteratively minimizing the state fitting error between the reference and real system, which can be adapted to various advanced RL methods.  Our evaluation results on three control tasks demonstrate that the proposed ITSRL framework can improve the performance of RL controller under perturbation.

feedback1: The numerical experiments are conducted only on small datasets. It is unclear how the method works on larger datasets such as ImageNet and, more importantly, how it compares with state-of-the-art pruning methods on more challenging datasets. In particular, no performance comparison is provided for the CIFAR10-VGG16 setting.
feedback2: Were the fine-tuning results in the random pruning experiments performed with random pruning or with your importance metric? For a fair comparison, the treatment of the pruned network should be the same in both cases. Did you try pruning with your method without retraining? The most drastic results should be shown with fine-tuning of both importance-pruned and randomly-pruned networks.

feedback1: The assumptions used in the theoretical analysis are not fully justified. Each assumption should be accompanied by a justification, explaining whether it is easy to fulfill in real-world scenarios. For instance, Assumption 1, which requires $e(t)$ to be bounded for infinite time $t$, might be a strong and hard-to-verify assumption in practice. Similarly, Equation (13) and the many assumptions in Theorem 2 also lack justification.
feedback2: Clarify that the assumption x(t) = hat x(t) + e(t) is an identity definition rather than an observed error assumption, and focus on bounding e(t) and dot e(t) in terms of modeling error and policy properties. Consider replacing Assumption 1 with more standard assumptions such as local Lipschitzness of f and hat f, bounded modeling error Delta(x,u), and Lipschitz or bounded policies.
title: Reinforcement Learning for Control with Stability Guarantee
abstract:

feedback1: It would be interesting to examine the correlation between the importance score and the actual impact on network performance. For instance, this could be done by comparing random pruning with pruning higher-importance connections. It might also be useful to examine the performance of networks after pruning nodes of differing importance without full retraining or just fine-tuning, as it is unclear how important full retraining is for this method.
feedback2: Were the fine-tuning results in the random pruning experiments performed with random pruning or with your importance metric? For a fair comparison, the treatment of the pruned network should be the same in both cases. Did you try pruning with your method without retraining? The most drastic results should be shown with fine-tuning of both importance-pruned and randomly-pruned networks.
title: Probabilistic Connection Importance Inference and Lossless Compression of Deep Neural Networks
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) can be huge in size, requiring a considerable a mount of energy and computational resources to operate, which limits their applications in numerous scenarios. It is thus of interest to compress DNNs while maintaining their performance levels.  We here propose a probabilistic importance inference approach for pruning DNNs. Specifically, we test the significance of the relevance of a connection in a DNN to the DNN’s outputs using a nonparemtric scoring testand keep only those significant ones. Experimental results show that the proposed approach achieves better lossless compression rates than existing techniques

feedback1: The authors do not include the searching cost and inference latency in Table 1. Different NAS papers have different objectives and searching cost. For instance, ProxylessNAS (Cai et al., 2019) and DenseNAS (Fang et al., 2019) focus on searching cost and require only 200 and 92 GPU hours (with TITAN XP). However, the proposed AtomNAS takes 32 * 25.5 = 816 GPU hours (with V100). The authors only point out that DenseNAS uses more parameters. It would be better if the authors can make the comparison more transparent.
feedback2: The search cost comparison is incomplete and misleading because the claimed "negligible searching cost" is based on a setup using 32 V100 GPUs, batch size 2048, and 350 epochs, which is not negligible in most NAS contexts and not directly comparable to claims of less than 4 hours search time.
title: AtomNAS: Fine-Grained End-to-End Neural Architecture Search
abstract:
Search space design is very critical to neural architecture search (NAS) algorithms. We propose a fine-grained search space comprised of atomic blocks, a minimal search unit that is much smaller than the ones used in recent NAS algorithms. This search space allows a mix of operations by composing different types of atomic blocks, while the search space in previous methods only allows homogeneous operations. Based on this search space, we propose a resource-aware architecture search framework which automatically assigns the computational resources (e.g., output channel numbers) for each operation by jointly considering the performance and the computational cost. In addition, to accelerate the search process, we propose a dynamic network shrinkage technique which prunes the atomic blocks with negligible influence on outputs on the fly.  Instead of a search-and-retrain two-stage paradigm, our method simultaneously searches and trains the target architecture. 
Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance under several FLOPs configurations on ImageNet with a small searching cost.
We open our entire codebase at: https://github.com/meijieru/AtomNAS.

feedback1: Were the fine-tuning results in the random pruning experiments performed with random pruning or with your importance metric? For a fair comparison, the treatment of the pruned network should be the same in both cases. Did you try pruning with your method without retraining? The most drastic results should be shown with fine-tuning of both importance-pruned and randomly-pruned networks.
feedback2: Regarding the random pruning experiments: Was 'Fine-tuning' performed using random pruning or the importance metric? When you found that random pruning could not achieve an LCR of less than one, was that with or without fine-tuning? The treatment of the pruned network, whether using random pruning or your importance metric, should be the same.
title: Probabilistic Connection Importance Inference and Lossless Compression of Deep Neural Networks
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) can be huge in size, requiring a considerable a mount of energy and computational resources to operate, which limits their applications in numerous scenarios. It is thus of interest to compress DNNs while maintaining their performance levels.  We here propose a probabilistic importance inference approach for pruning DNNs. feedback1: There is a typo on page 2, in the second paragraph above the Related Works: 'oin' should be 'in'.
feedback2: The concept of 'stable representation' is central to the paper but is not formally defined or directly measured in the experiments.
title: Lmser-pix2seq: Learning Stable Sketch Representations For Sketch Healing
abstract: Sketch healing aims to recreate a complete sketch from the corrupted one. The sparse and abstract nature of the sketch makes it challenging due to the difficulty in learning. The features extracted from the corrupted sketch may be inconsistent with the ones from the corresponding full sketch. In this paper, we present Lmser-pix2seq to learn stable sketch representations against the missing information by employing a Least mean square error reconstruction (Lmser) block, which falls into encoder-decoder paradigm. Taking as input a corrupted sketch, the Lmser encoder computes the embeddings of structural patterns of the input, while the decoder reconstructs the complete sketch from the embeddings. We build bi-directional skip connections between the encoder and the decoder in our Lmser block. The feedback connections enable recurrent paths to receive more information about the reconstructed sketch produced by the decoder, which helps the encoder extract stable sketch features. The features captured by the Lmser block are eventually fed into a recurrent neural network decoder to recreate the sketches. Experimental results show that our Lmser-pix2seq outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in sketch healing, especially when the sketches are heavily masked or corrupted.

feedback1: From Eqn.(1) to Eqn.(5), you choose to use a complex gating mechanism to aggregate information. Is every component necessary? What if using a simple GCN or GAT?
feedback2: The authors should provide some real cases to show how the method outperforms previous baselines, and why the method can obtain good results without templates.
title: Permutation invariant graph-to-sequence model for template-free retrosynthesis and reaction prediction
abstract: Synthesis planning and reaction outcome prediction are two fundamental problems in computer-aided organic chemistry for which a variety of data-driven approaches have emerged. Natural language approaches that model each problem as a SMILES-to-SMILES translation lead to a simple end-to-end formulation, reduce the need for data preprocessing, and enable the use of well-optimized machine translation model architectures. However, SMILES representations are not an efficient representation for capturing information about molecular structure, as evidenced by the success of SMILES augmentation to boost empirical performance. Here, we describe a novel Graph2SMILES model that combines the power of Transformer models for text generation with the permutation invariance of molecular graph encoders. As an end-to-end architecture, Graph2SMILES can be used as a drop-in replacement for the Transformer in any task involving molecule(s)-to-molecule(s) transformations. In our encoder, an attention-augmented directed message passing neural network (D-MPNN) captures local chemical environments, and the global attention encoder allows for long-range and intermolecular interactions, enhanced by graph-aware positional embedding.  Graph2SMILES improves the top-1 accuracy of the Transformer baselines by $1.7\%$ and $1.9\%$ for reaction outcome prediction on USPTO_480k and USPTO_STEREO datasets respectively, and by $9.8\%$ for one-step retrosynthesis on the USPTO_50k dataset.

feedback1: I encourage the authors to release the code, the new Ret metric, and the revised data feeding mentioned in section 4.1 to aid reproducibility.
feedback2: The paper presents inconsistent descriptions of the corruption process used for sketch healing, which affects the fairness of comparisons to baselines and clarity of the experimental setup.
title: Lmser-pix2seq: Learning Stable Sketch Representations For Sketch Healing
abstract: Sketch healing aims to recreate a complete sketch from the corrupted one. The sparse and abstract nature of the sketch makes it challenging due to the difficulty in learning. The features extracted from the corrupted sketch may be inconsistent with the ones from the corresponding full sketch. In this paper, we present Lmser-pix2seq to learn stable sketch representations against the missing information by employing a Least mean square error reconstruction (Lmser) block, which falls into encoder-decoder paradigm. Taking as input a corrupted sketch, the Lmser encoder computes the embeddings of structural patterns of the input, while the decoder reconstructs the complete sketch from the embeddings. We build bi-directional skip connections between the encoder and the decoder in our Lmser block. The feedback connections enable recurrent paths to receive more information about the reconstructed sketch produced by the decoder, which helps the encoder extract stable sketch features. The features captured by the Lmser block are eventually fed into a recurrent neural network decoder to recreate the sketches. Experimental results show that our Lmser-pix2seq outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in sketch healing, especially when the sketches are heavily masked or corrupted.

feedback1: The results in Table 1 are only convincing for USPTO_STEREO_mixed and less so for other datasets.
feedback2: How exactly is \mathcal{B}_{u,v} used in the learnable \tilde{r}_{u,v}?

feedback1: The differences between sketch-pix2seq, RPCL-pix2seq, and Lmser-pix2seq are not clear.
feedback2: The paper does not clearly articulate the novelty and contributions of the LMSER block relative to existing pix2seq variants, which may cause confusion about the significance of the work.

feedback1: It is still not clear how the minor modifications from NERF impact the performance of the proposed method, either theoretically or empirically.
feedback2: The model contains more parameters than conventional retrosynthesis models like conventional Transformer, GLN. Could you please compare the number of parameters of different methods?

feedback1: The paper should include more comprehensive ablation studies about the weight-learning strategy (e.g., C-NET) and the effect of the PooledStudent logits.
feedback2: The paper should define more carefully what is meant by 'state-of-the-art baselines' and include several strong KD baselines such as FitNets, Attention Transfer, CRD, RKD, DKD, and KD with label smoothing, or explicitly justify the selection if focusing only on certain categories of baselines.
title: Meta-Collaboration in Distillation: Pooled Learning from Multiple Students
abstract: Knowledge distillation (KD) approximates a large teacher model using a smaller student model. KD can be used to train multiple students of different capacities, allowing for flexible management of inference costs at test time. We propose a novel distillation method we term meta-collaboration, wherein a set of students are simultaneously distilled from a single teacher and can improve each other through information sharing during distillation. We model this information sharing through a separate network designed to predict instance-specific loss mixing for each of the students. This auxiliary network is trained jointly with the multi-student distillation, utilizing a separate meta-loss aggregating student model loss on a separate validation set. Our method improves student accuracy for all students and beats to state-of-the-art distillation baselines, including methods that use multi-step distillation, combining models of different sizes. In particular, addition of smaller students to the pool clearly benefits larger student models, through the mechanism of meta-collaboration. We show average gains of 2.5\% on CIFAR100 \& 2\% on TinyImageNet datasets; our gains are consistent across a wide range of student sizes, teacher sizes, and model architectures.

feedback1: The proposed framework is similar to the NERF approach (Bi et al. ICML 2021). Both use graph neural networks to capture local information and a Transformer encoder for intermolecular interactions, followed by a Transformer decoder. The main difference is that NERF decodes directly to a graph, while the proposed method decodes to SMILES strings. Therefore, the novelty of this paper seems limited.
feedback2: Dual-view Molecule Pre-training (https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.10234) also works on retrosynthesis using Transformer and GNN models. A comparison is necessary.

feedback1: The test statistics resemble HSIC. It would be helpful to include comments on the connection to HSIC.
feedback2: Explicitly position your method against closely related dependence measures such as HSIC and MMD, clarifying how your statistic differs from HSIC between (α,β) and Y, why you remove main effects via ANOVA-style decomposition, and whether your method is essentially an HSIC on an interaction-only kernel.
title: Probabilistic Connection Importance Inference and Lossless Compression of Deep Neural Networks
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) can be huge in size, requiring a considerable a mount of energy and computational resources to operate, which limits their applications in numerous scenarios. It is thus of interest to compress DNNs while maintaining their performance levels.  We here propose a probabilistic importance inference approach for pruning DNNs. Specifically, we test the significance of the relevance of a connection in a DNN to the DNN’s outputs using a nonparemtric scoring testand keep only those significant ones. Experimental results show that the proposed approach achieves better lossless compression rates than existing techniques

feedback1: In the experimental comparison for retrosynthesis, methods compared use different forms of pretraining or augmentation, and while augmentation still provides advantages, the graph-based molecule embedding may mean that SMILES augmentation would not further improve your results.
feedback2: The authors claim that integrating features like reaction templates, atom mapping, pretraining, or data augmentation with Graph2SMILES could improve its performance, but do not conduct experiments to demonstrate this claim. As these techniques are common in predictive chemistry, the authors should explain why they did not equip Graph2SMILES with these techniques.

feedback1: The claim in the Abstract that 'molecular graph encoders mitigate the need for input data augmentation' is very strong, but there is no evidence to support it. Slightly better performance over the SMILES-to-SMILES transformer baseline is not convincing. Input data augmentation may play a significant role in regularizing deep neural networks. Better justification for this claim is necessary.
feedback2: Improve Figure 1 readability by adding explicit labels indicating where positional embeddings enter the attention score, where atom representations are pooled or maintained, and how multiple molecules are handled. Consider including a small example reaction graph illustrating bucket assignments.

feedback1: The experiments do not fully show the model's impact, especially regarding the lack of augmentation needed at inference time.
feedback2: Augmentation at training time is not really an issue and yields good performance, but the need for augmentation during inference time might be the more interesting aspect and should be stressed more, possibly shown experimentally with the same pretraining.

feedback1: Have you considered using more than two temperatures in your method?
feedback2: The operator E is not defined in the main text (only in the appendix) for Theorem 1.
title: Accelerating Convergence of Replica Exchange Stochastic Gradient MCMC via Variance Reduction
abstract: Replica exchange stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics (reSGLD) has shown promise in accelerating the convergence in non-convex learning; however, an excessively large correction for avoiding biases from noisy energy estimators has limited the potential of the acceleration. To address this issue, we study the variance reduction for noisy energy estimators, which promotes much more effective swaps. Theoretically, we provide a non-asymptotic analysis on the exponential convergence for the underlying continuous-time Markov jump process; moreover, we consider a generalized Girsanov theorem which includes the change of Poisson measure to overcome the crude discretization based on the Gr\"{o}wall's inequality and yields a much tighter error in the 2-Wasserstein ($\mathcal{W}_2$) distance. Numerically, we conduct extensive experiments and obtain state-of-the-art results in optimization and uncertainty estimates for synthetic experiments and image data.

feedback1: The claim that 'we guarantee the permutation invariance of Graph2SMILES to the input, eliminating the need for input-side augmentation altogether' is unclear. It would be useful to specify how and why permutation invariance is achieved.
feedback2: Clarify precisely what is meant by 'permutation invariance' in the model, specifying that the encoder is permutation-invariant to node ordering but the overall model is not permutation-invariant end-to-end due to the decoder outputting an order-dependent SMILES sequence.

feedback1: Please define 'acceleration' (relative to what?) and clarify what is meant by 'exponentially faster' on page 5, compared to reSGLD.
feedback2: The novelty and originality of the paper are limited, as a well-known type of variance reduction is applied in a new context where it makes sense.

feedback1: On page 7: Why do you anneal $\tau^{(1)}$ in the CIFAR experiment?
feedback2: The proposed variance reduction technique is not new and is very similar to standard control variates methods, which have already been extensively applied to MCMCs and other machine learning tasks.

feedback1: It is unclear whether the proposed attention in the GNN is useful, as there is only a small advantage of using D-GAT over D-GCN and the ablation results are missing.
feedback2: Clarify the parameterization and computational fairness between the D-GAT and D-GCN variants, including reporting parameter counts if D-GAT adds parameters, and provide a complexity discussion comparing D-MPNN (O(E) per layer) and global attention (O(N²)) to highlight implications for large reactions.

feedback1: On page 4, Algorithm 1: How should $\gamma$ be set?
feedback2: For the synthetic experiments, can you explain the choice of using different values of F (1 for VR-reSGLD and 100 for reSGLD)? While I can believe that variance reduction leads to a different bias/variance trade-off, it would be good to explain why these specific values of F were used.

feedback1: What is the role of the thinning factor T in the algorithm? Is it involved in the convergence guarantee given by Theorem 3?
feedback2: Discuss the computational feasibility of periodically evaluating the full energy over the entire dataset, including reporting wall-clock and GPU memory overheads in the main experiments section, and consider alternative approaches such as subsampled control variates or cached per-example losses.

feedback1: How much tweaking do parameters such as $F$ and other hyperparameters require? Can you provide some intuition on how to set these parameters?
feedback2: In Section 5.2, regarding Figures 3c and 3d, the effect of variance reduction becomes significant after sufficient epochs, and the author conjectures this is because the learning rate is decreased. I think this suggests the proposed variance reduction is significantly affected by the step size, which changes during the exploration stage in SG-MCMCs. Therefore, using a constant $m$ during the exploration stage might not be a good idea.
title: Accelerating Convergence of Replica Exchange Stochastic Gradient MCMC via Variance Reduction
abstract: Replica exchange stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics (reSGLD) has shown promise in accelerating the convergence in non-convex learning; however, an excessively large correction for avoiding biases from noisy energy estimators has limited the potential of the acceleration. To address this issue, we study the variance reduction for noisy energy estimators, which promotes much more effective swaps. Theoretically, we provide a non-asymptotic analysis on the exponential convergence for the underlying continuous-time Markov jump process; moreover, we consider a generalized Girsanov theorem which includes the change of Poisson measure to overcome the crude discretization based on the Gr\"{o}wall's inequality and yields a much tighter error in the 2-Wasserstein ($\mathcal{W}_2$) distance. Numerically, we conduct extensive experiments and obtain state-of-the-art results in optimization and uncertainty estimates for synthetic experiments and image data.

feedback1: Instead of only using definition 1, I recommend the authors directly specify that they are looking at example-level privacy by adding a definition similar to Def 2 in Lowy et al. or Def 3.1 in Liu et al. The same applies to Theorem H.1; without clearly stating the privacy notion, it is confusing for readers to understand what level of privacy is protected, especially with clustering.
feedback2: Define the clustering accuracy metric precisely, specifying which standard metric (e.g., ARI, NMI, purity) is used, how label permutation is handled, and at which rounds clustering accuracy is measured.
title: A Provably Robust Algorithm for Differentially Private Clustered Federated Learning
abstract: Federated Learning (FL), which is a decentralized machine learning (ML) approach, often incorporates differential privacy (DP) to enhance data privacy guarantees. However, differentially private federated learning (DPFL) introduces performance disparities across clients, particularly affecting minority groups. Some recent works have attempted to address large data heterogeneity in vanilla FL settings through clustering clients, but these methods remain sensitive and prone to errors further exacerbated by the DP noise, making them inappropriate for DPFL settings. We propose an algorithm for differentially private clustered FL, which is robust to the DP noise in the system and identifies clients’ clusters correctly. To this end, we propose to cluster clients based on both their model updates and training loss values. Furthermore, when clustering clients’ model updates, our proposed approach addresses the server’s uncertainties by employing large batch sizes as well as Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) to reduce the impact of DP and stochastic noise and avoid potential clustering errors. This idea is efficient especially in privacy-sensitive scenarios with more DP noise. We provide theoretical analysis justifying our approach, and evaluate it extensively across diverse data distributions and privacy budgets. Our experimental results show its effectiveness in addressing large data heterogeneity in DPFL systems with a small computational cost.

feedback1: For the synthetic experiments, can you explain the choice of using different values of F (1 for VR-reSGLD and 100 for reSGLD)? While I can believe that variance reduction leads to a different bias/variance trade-off, it would be good to explain why these specific values of F were used.
feedback2: All experimental results and theoretical analysis suggest that using a smaller $m$ is better. However, how should $m$ be tuned? Does the variance reduction work even if $m=1$? I also think that a sufficiently large $m$ is required to estimate the control covariate appropriately, as suggested by Rie Johnson and Tong Zhang in their paper. Is this intuition incorrect?

feedback1: It looks like Liu et al. has a DP clustered federated learning method. Could the authors briefly explain the difference and the advantage of the proposed method?
feedback2: Provide detailed derivation for Lemma 4.1 explaining the variance scaling, including the number of minibatches per epoch, composition across steps, and separation of stochastic and DP noise terms, to justify the unusual \(1/b^3\) scaling.

feedback1: When switching to loss-based clustering in Algorithm 1 (line 21), how is differential privacy (DP) guaranteed, or if this is required? There are no details provided on how exactly this part works.
feedback2: Precisely define the weights \(w_i^e\) used in aggregation and clarify how model broadcast and client assignment are performed, including whether weights are proportional to client dataset sizes and normalized within clusters, especially considering their interaction with utility and fairness under differential privacy.

feedback1: It would be good to extend the discussion to model-based exploration agents, such as the works in [1], [2], and [3] that use Dreamer/RSSM-based agents for visual control and ensemble disagreement for exploration.
feedback2: The claim of closed-form KL divergence for tanh-squashed Gaussians is subtle and potentially incorrect due to practical implementation details like clipping and numerical corrections.
title: Decoupled Actor-Critic
abstract: Actor-Critic methods are in a stalemate of two seemingly irreconcilable problems. Firstly, critic proneness towards overestimation requires sampling temporal-difference targets from a conservative policy optimized using lower-bound Q-values. Secondly, well-known results show that policies that are optimistic in the face of uncertainty yield lower regret levels. To remedy this dichotomy, we propose Decoupled Actor-Critic (DAC). DAC is an off-policy algorithm that learns two distinct actors by gradient backpropagation: a conservative actor used for temporal-difference learning and an optimistic actor used for exploration. We test DAC on DeepMind Control tasks in low and high replay ratio regimes and ablate multiple design choices. Despite minimal computational overhead, DAC achieves state-of-the-art performance and sample efficiency on locomotion tasks.

feedback1: All experimental results and theoretical analysis suggest that using a smaller $m$ is better. However, how should $m$ be tuned? Does the variance reduction work even if $m=1$? I also think that a sufficiently large $m$ is required to estimate the control covariate appropriately, as suggested by Rie Johnson and Tong Zhang in their paper. Is this intuition incorrect?
feedback2: In Section 5.2, regarding Figures 3c and 3d, the effect of variance reduction becomes significant after sufficient epochs, and the author conjectures this is because the learning rate is decreased. I think this suggests the proposed variance reduction is significantly affected by the step size, which changes during the exploration stage in SG-MCMCs. Therefore, using a constant $m$ during the exploration stage might not be a good idea.

abstract: Replica exchange stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics (reSGLD) has shown promise in accelerating the convergence in non-convex learning; however, an excessively large correction for avoiding biases from noisy energy estimators has limited the potential of the acceleration. feedback1: The relationship between high entropy and large loss is unclear. While high-uncertainty or large-loss samples are commonly considered noisy in noisy label learning, the proof claims that high-uncertainty samples are SCF samples. What is the relationship between high entropy and large loss?
feedback2: Explain more directly in the main paper why loss-based selection picks more noisy labels under symmetric noise, possibly including a toy plot or analytic example illustrating the enrichment factor.
title: Improving group robustness under noisy labels using predictive uncertainty
abstract: The standard empirical risk minimization (ERM) can underperform on certain minority groups (i.e., waterbirds in lands or landbirds in water) due to the spurious correlation between the input and its label. Several studies have improved the worst-group accuracy by focusing on the high-loss samples. The hypothesis behind this is that such high-loss samples are spurious-cue-free (SCF) samples. However, these approaches can be problematic since the high-loss samples may also be samples with noisy labels in the real-world scenarios. To resolve this issue, we utilize the predictive uncertainty of a model to improve the worst-group accuracy under noisy labels. To motivate this, we theoretically show that the high-uncertainty samples are the SCF samples in the binary classification problem. This theoretical result implies that the predictive uncertainty is an adequate indicator to identify SCF samples in a noisy label setting. Motivated from this, we propose a novel Entropy based Debiasing (END) framework that prevents models from learning the spurious cues while being robust to the noisy labels. In the END framework, we first train the \textit{identification model} to obtain the SCF samples from a training set using its predictive uncertainty. Then, another model is trained on the dataset augmented with an oversampled SCF set. The experimental results show that our END framework outperforms other strong baselines on several real-world benchmarks that consider both the noisy labels and the spurious-cues.

feedback1: Most results do not report any deviation measure besides the mean.
feedback2: Your neighborhood definition on lines 133-134 is now 'replace' neighborhood, while elsewhere you state you use 'add/remove'. Please fix this inconsistency.

feedback1: Why did the authors only apply the proposed pre-training objectives on top of ELECTRA instead of standard MLM, such as RoBERTa? The latter should be a better testbed for objectives targeting problems in MLM.
feedback2: Soften the novelty claims by acknowledging related work on label smoothing, semantic similarity losses, multiple references, and synonym-aware training, and add a paragraph contrasting the proposed method with these prior approaches.
title: Language Model Pre-training on True Negatives
abstract:
Discriminative pre-trained language models (PrLMs) learn to predict original texts from intentionally corrupted ones. Taking the former text as positive and the latter as negative samples, the discriminative PrLM can be trained effectively for contextualized representation. However, though the training of such a type of PrLMs highly relies on the quality of the automatically constructed samples, existing PrLMs simply treat all corrupted texts as equal negative without any examination, which actually lets the resulting model inevitably suffer from the false negative issue where training is carried out on wrong data and leads to less efficiency and less robustness in the resulting PrLMs.
Thus in this work, on the basis of defining the false negative issue in discriminative PrLMs that has been ignored for a long time, we design enhanced pre-training methods to counteract false negative predictions and encourage pre-training language models on true negatives, by correcting the harmful gradient updates subject to false negative predictions. Experimental results on GLUE and SQuAD benchmarks show that our counter-false-negative pre-training methods indeed bring about better performance together with stronger robustness.

feedback1: Your neighborhood definition on lines 133-134 is now 'replace' neighborhood, while elsewhere you state you use 'add/remove'. Please fix this inconsistency.
feedback2: Theorem 4.3 relies on a strong assumption that in the first round, model updates from all clients are i.i.d. samples from a Gaussian mixture. Is this assumption well justified, and is it a standard or realistic assumption in practical settings?

feedback1: The experiments only cover one pretrained language model - ELECTRA. Could this method be applied on other MLM PrLMs such as RoBERTa?
feedback2: Clarify the meaning of “false negatives” separately for ELECTRA’s discriminator replaced-token detection loss and the generator MLM loss, explicitly defining how false negatives arise in each and stating which loss(es) the proposed SR/HC methods modify.

feedback2: Some experimental settings are not clearly described, particularly how to choose the time to switch from update-based GMM soft clustering to loss-based hard clustering. How does the server determine and set hyperparameters of the Gaussian mixture model, such as the number of clusters? I would like to see how different choices of these parameters (switching round and number of clusters) affect the results.

feedback1: The motivation of this paper is not very convincing. While I agree with the importance of ensuring auto-constructed data is accurate, how can we measure the severity of the 'false negative problem'? For example, in Table 1, both 'Ground-truth' and 'Prediction' could fit the masked tokens, but there are subtle differences, especially in long contexts (512/1024 tokens during pretraining vs. a short sentence in the examples).
feedback2: Define and measure the false negative rate directly by quantifying the percentage of masked positions where top-k predictions contain synonyms or paraphrases of the gold token, report how often HC triggers during training, and show correlation between trigger rate and downstream performance gains.

feedback1: In figure 1, the motivation for using smaller batch sizes in the third stage is unclear. Can a fixed batch size be used throughout training? In practical implementations of (centralized) DP-SGD, people usually use very large batch sizes to improve performance. Why should clients switch to a smaller batch size?
feedback2: Switching the batch size changes the sample rate of the training data, which could lead to issues with privacy composition. To my knowledge, such functionality is unavailable in popular Python DP libraries like Opacus or fastDP.
title: A Provably Robust Algorithm for Differentially Private Clustered Federated Learning
abstract: Federated Learning (FL), which is a decentralized machine learning (ML) approach, often incorporates differential privacy (DP) to enhance data privacy guarantees. feedback1: The motivation for proposing the LDIFF metric to identify which layers can be pruned is unclear in Section 5. Please clarify why the LDIFF metric is designed to avoid pruning layers with a majority of non-discriminative filters, and consider visualizing and analyzing the fraction of discriminative filters in each layer.
feedback2: The definition and interpretation of LDIFF, particularly its formula \(4\tau(1-\tau)\), have an unclear connection to pruneability and contain contradictory intuitions about pruning layers with no discriminative filters.
title: TVSPrune - Pruning Non-discriminative filters via Total Variation separability of intermediate representations without fine tuning
abstract: Achieving structured, data-free sparsity of deep neural networks (DNNs) remains an open area of research.  In this work, we address the challenge of pruning filters without access to the original training set or loss function. We propose the discriminative filters hypothesis, that well-trained models possess discriminative filters, and any non-discriminative filters can be pruned without impacting the predictive performance of the classifier. Based on this hypothesis, we propose a new paradigm for pruning neural networks: distributional pruning, wherein we only require access to the distributions that generated the original datasets. Our approach to solving the problem of formalising and quantifying the discriminating ability of filters is through the total variation (TV) distance between the class-conditional distributions of the filter outputs. We present empirical results that, using this definition of discriminability, support our hypothesis on a variety of datasets and architectures. Next, we define the LDIFF score, a heuristic to quantify the extent to which a layer possesses a mixture of discriminative and non-discriminative filters. We empirically demonstrate that the LDIFF score is indicative of the performance of random pruning for a given layer, and thereby indicates the extent to which a layer may be pruned. Our main contribution is a novel one-shot pruning algorithm, called TVSPrune, that identifies non-discriminative filters for pruning. We extend this algorithm to IterTVSPrune, wherein we iteratively apply TVSPrune, thereby enabling us to achieve greater sparsity. Last, we demonstrate the efficacy of the TVSPrune on a variety of datasets, and show that in some cases, we can prune up to 60% of parameters with only a 2% loss of accuracy without any fine-tuning of the model, beating the nearest baseline by almost 10%.

feedback1: The authors claim that the proposed method is private, but it is unclear what level of privacy is enforced and what the threat model is in this work. It appears that the notion of privacy being used is example-level privacy because DP-SGD is used and the goal is to protect the server/other clients within the same cluster from inferring information of an example. If that is the case, shouldn't the clustering process itself also be privatized?
feedback2: There are no theoretical results of the DP bound for the proposed method. Additionally, due to the lack of a formal threat model, it is unclear what privacy guarantee the proposed method provides.
title: A Provably Robust Algorithm for Differentially Private Clustered Federated Learning
abstract: Federated Learning (FL), which is a decentralized machine learning (ML) approach, often incorporates differential privacy (DP) to enhance data privacy guarantees. However, differentially private federated learning (DPFL) introduces performance disparities across clients, particularly affecting minority groups. Some recent works have attempted to address large data heterogeneity in vanilla FL settings through clustering clients, but these methods remain sensitive and prone to errors further exacerbated by the DP noise, making them inappropriate for DPFL settings. Our experimental results show its effectiveness in addressing large data heterogeneity in DPFL systems with a small computational cost.

feedback1: The authors assert that the learning of conflicting tokens increases the loss of most other tokens within the same expert. This is counterintuitive since conflicting tokens should be relatively few, so the directions of parameter optimization should align with the global average gradient of all tokens in the expert. Clarifying this assumption would improve the paper's clarity.
feedback2: The paper is ambiguous about which gradients are used for conflict detection; it should specify whether per-token loss gradients or total loss gradients attributed per token are computed, and clarify which tokens are included in gradient computation.
title: Solving Token Gradient Conflict in Mixture-of-Experts for Large Vision-Language Model
abstract:
The Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has gained increasing attention in studying Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). It uses a sparse model to replace the dense model, achieving comparable performance while activating fewer parameters during inference, thus significantly reducing the inference cost. Existing MoE methods in LVLM encourage different experts to specialize in different tokens, and they usually employ a router to predict the routing of each token. However, the router is not optimized concerning distinct parameter optimization directions generated from tokens within an expert. This may lead to severe interference between tokens within an expert. To address this problem, we propose to use the token-level gradient analysis to Solving Token Gradient Conflict (STGC) in this paper. Specifically, we first use token-level gradients to identify conflicting tokens in experts. After that, we add a regularization loss tailored to encourage conflicting tokens routing from their current experts to other experts, for reducing interference between tokens within an expert. Our method can serve as a plug-in for diverse LVLM methods, and extensive experimental results demonstrate its effectiveness. demonstrate its effectiveness. 
The code will be publicly available at https://github.com/longrongyang/STGC.

feedback1: If a client directly uses the best loss over the local training data to choose which model to use, this choice can leak out information beyond what is allowed by DPSGD, leading to invalid DP guarantees.
feedback2: If clients select models locally and send this selection to the server, this model selection can leak information about the local data distribution, potentially breaking DP guarantees. Even if the average train loss changes slightly due to a record-level change, the model selection could still reveal information.

feedback1: Even though the generated target domain is supposed to represent the 'worst-case', it is still not the same as the true target domains. It would be better to discuss this distinction in the theoretical analysis.
feedback2: Provide ablation experiments without SWAD on OfficeHome and DomainNet datasets to isolate the contribution of AGFA independently of SWAD.
title: Domain Generalisation via Domain Adaptation: An Adversarial Fourier Amplitude Approach
abstract: We tackle the domain generalisation (DG) problem by posing it as a domain adaptation (DA) task where we adversarially synthesise the worst-case `target' domain and adapt a model to that worst-case domain, thereby improving the model’s robustness. To synthesise data that is challenging yet semantics-preserving, we generate Fourier amplitude images and combine them with source domain phase images, exploiting the widely believed conjecture from signal processing that amplitude spectra mainly determines image style, while phase data mainly captures image semantics. To synthesise a worst-case domain for adaptation, we train the classifier and the amplitude generator adversarially. Specifically, we exploit the maximum classifier discrepancy (MCD) principle from DA that relates the target domain performance to the discrepancy of classifiers in the model hypothesis space. By Bayesian hypothesis modeling, we express the model hypothesis space effectively as a posterior distribution over classifiers given the source domains, making adversarial MCD minimisation feasible. On the DomainBed benchmark including the large-scale DomainNet dataset, the proposed approach yields significantly improved domain generalisation performance over the state-of-the-art.

feedback1: When switching to loss-based clustering in Algorithm 1 (line 21), how is differential privacy (DP) guaranteed, or if this is required? There are no details provided on how exactly this part works.
feedback2: The authors claim that the proposed method is private, but it is unclear what level of privacy is enforced and what the threat model is in this work. It appears that the notion of privacy being used is example-level privacy because DP-SGD is used and the goal is to protect the server/other clients within the same cluster from inferring information of an example. If that is the case, shouldn't the clustering process itself also be privatized?
title: A Provably Robust Algorithm for Differentially Private Clustered Federated Learning
abstract: Federated Learning (FL), which is a decentralized machine learning (ML) approach, often incorporates differential privacy (DP) to enhance data privacy guarantees. However, differentially private federated learning (DPFL) introduces performance disparities across clients, particularly affecting minority groups. Some recent works have attempted to address large data heterogeneity in vanilla FL settings through clustering clients, but these methods remain sensitive and prone to errors further exacerbated by the DP noise, making them inappropriate for DPFL settings. We propose an algorithm for differentially private clustered FL, which is robust to the DP noise in the system and identifies clients’ clusters correctly. To this end, we propose to cluster clients based on both their model updates and training loss values. Furthermore, when clustering clients’ model updates, our proposed approach addresses the server’s uncertainties by employing large batch sizes as well as Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) to reduce the impact of DP and stochastic noise and avoid potential clustering errors. This idea is efficient especially in privacy-sensitive scenarios with more DP noise. We provide theoretical analysis justifying our approach, and evaluate it extensively across diverse data distributions and privacy budgets. Our experimental results show its effectiveness in addressing large data heterogeneity in DPFL systems with a small computational cost.

feedback1: The paper should provide more insightful discussions, for example, on how to improve the generalization ability of deep models using other adversarial DA approaches such as DANN and MDD.
feedback2: Evaluate test-time posterior sampling by comparing performance using posterior means versus sampling multiple weights and ensembling, to assess if ensembling improves results.

feedback1: When switching to loss-based clustering in Algorithm 1 (line 21), how is differential privacy (DP) guaranteed, or if this is required? There are no details provided on how exactly this part works.
feedback2: Please state how you implement the DP version of loss-based clustering methods, as loss values are sensitive and require an extra DP mechanism to release them.
